original_abstract:
We report a detailed mechanistic investigation of anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr⁺). Through transient absorption spectroscopy, we directly detected alkene cation radical intermediates, confirming a single-electron transfer pathway rather than exciplex formation. Laser flash photolysis studies further demonstrated that the phenylthiyl radical rapidly oxidizes the persistent acridinyl radical (Mes-Acr•) with a second-order rate constant of $3.1 \times 10^9 \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, a key step that synchronizes the photoredox and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) catalytic cycles. 

We also show that diphenyl disulfide (($\text{PhS}$)$_2$) functions as an effective HAT cocatalyst by undergoing photolytic S–S bond cleavage to enter the same catalytic manifold as thiophenol ($\text{PhSH}$). DFT calculations reveal that $\text{PhSH}$ is a significantly more kineticially efficient H-atom donor than 2-phenylmalononitrile ($\text{PMN}$), owing to lower structural reorganization energies in the transition state. Finally, we observed that Mes-Acr⁺ undergoes reversible off-cycle adduct formation during the early stages of the reaction, likely triggered by the accumulation of thiolate ($\text{PhS}^-$). Kinetic analysis suggests that this adduct formation, alongside the proton transfer step, exerts significant rate-limiting influence on the overall transformation.
humanized_abstract:
Anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization is a burgeoning catalytic transformation that holds significant promise in petroleum-derived petroleum-based feedstocks and renewable resources. Despite its emergent yet considerable importance, the structural pathway(s) underlying transition metal-free photoredox catalytic processes has not yet been fully clarified and remains subject to some debate. Herein, we report unequivocal insight into the structural catalytic pathways underlying the transition metal-free anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by the photocatalyst 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr⁺). Notably, the alkene cation radical species can be clearly observed by means of transient absorption spectroscopy, which strongly supports the single-electron transfer pathway over that of the previously-accepted exciplex mechanism. Laser flash photolysis investigations into Mes-Acr⁺-induced signal-generating photochemistry revealed that the phenylthiyl radical could rapidly oxidize the persistently-existing Mes-Acr• species with a second-order rate constant of $3.1 \times 10^9 \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, and accordingly the rapid oxidation between the Mes-Acr• species is regarded as the coupling catalyst cycle between photoredox and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT).

In situ generated 2-phenylmalononitrile (PMN) functions as the H-atom acceptor that enables protonation of Mes-Acr⁻. We also demonstrate that diphenyl disulfide ((PhS)2) can act as a HAT cocatalyst. Upon photolysis, (PhS)2 can be cleaved into (PhS)·, which shares the same reactive manifold as thiophenol (PhSH). Our DFT calculations confirmed that PhSH is indeed a more efficient H-atom donor than PMN due to lower energy barriers attributed to lesser structural distortions in the transition states. Mes-Acr⁺ also engages in reversible adduct binding in the off-cycle in the early stages of the reaction that we believe is initiated by thiolate binding PhS⁻. Analysis of the kinetic data indicated that the adduct binding and proton transfer steps possess considerable rate determining character to the catalytic reaction.

document.output:
Anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization is a burgeoning catalytic transformation that holds significant promise in petroleum-derived petroleum-based feedstocks and renewable resources. Despite its emergent yet considerable importance, the structural pathway(s) underlying transition metal-free photoredox catalytic processes has not yet been fully clarified and remains subject to some debate. Analysis of the kinetic data indicated that the adduct binding and proton transfer steps possess considerable rate determining character to the catalytic reaction.
document.input:
We report a detailed mechanistic investigation of anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr⁺). Kinetic analysis suggests that this adduct formation, alongside the proton transfer step, exerts significant rate-limiting influence on the overall transformation.
